name: The World Tree: Stories from the North
description:
Forty-two Norse myths retold as rhyming bedtime verses for children, spanning from the void of Ginnungagap to the green world reborn after Ragnarök. This is the ONLY major mythology where the gods LOSE — Odin falls, Thor dies, the world burns — and then it begins again, green and new. That cycle of ending-and-beginning makes Norse myth uniquely honest about loss and uniquely hopeful about renewal.

Sources: Snorri Sturluson's Prose Edda (c. 1220), the Poetic Edda / Elder Edda (13th century Codex Regius), and R.B. Anderson's Norse Mythology (1875).

PUBLIC DOMAIN ILLUSTRATION REFERENCES: W.G. Collingwood's illustrations for the Prose Edda translation by Olive Bray (1908) — stark black-and-white ink drawings of Odin, Thor, and the World Tree. Arthur Rackham's Ring Cycle illustrations for Wagner (1910-1911) — atmospheric watercolors of gods, giants, and twilight. Lorenz Frølich's Norse mythology illustrations (1895) — detailed engravings of Eddic scenes. Viking Age carvings and runestones, especially the Gotland picture stones depicting Odin's horse Sleipnir and scenes from Ragnarök.

sections.Story:
Before the stars, before the light,
Before the day, before the night,
There was a GAP so wide and deep —
A YAWNING VOID where shadows sleep.

No up, no down, no ground, no sky,
No wind to blow, no bird to fly,
Just GINNUNGAGAP, the empty space,
A silence waiting to embrace.

But nothing doesn't stay for long —
The void was humming with a SONG,
A rumble in the deepest dark
That soon would light the very first SPARK.
sections.Wonder: What do you think was there before everything? Can you imagine a place with no light, no sound, no ground to stand on? Close your eyes and try.
sections.Whisper: Ginnungagap ('gaping void') appears in Völuspá stanza 3 and Gylfaginning chapter 5. The Old Norse concept isn't simply 'nothing' — it's a charged emptiness, a potentiality. Scholars like Mircea Eliade connect it to the universal motif of creative chaos preceding cosmic order. The void is not absence but pregnant possibility.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

"He hath formed," added Jafnhar, "heaven and earth, and the air, and all things thereunto belonging."

"And what is more," continued Thridi, "he hath made man, and given him a soul which shall live and never perish though the body shall have mouldered away, or have been burnt to ashes. And all that are righteous shall dwell with him in the place called Gimli, or Vingolf; but the wicked shall go to Hel, and thence to Niflhel, which is below, in the ninth world."

"And where did this god remain before he made heaven and earth?" demanded Gangler.

"He was then," replied Har, "with the Hrimthursar."[126]

OF THE PRIMORDIAL STATE OF THE UNIVERSE.

4. "But with what did he begin, or what was the beginning of things?" demanded Gangler.

"Hear," replied Har, "what is said in the Voluspa."

"'Twas time's first dawn, When nought yet was, Nor sand nor sea, Nor cooling wave; Earth was not there,

Nor heaven above. Nought save a void And yawning gulf.

metadata.archetype: The Primordial Void — chaos before creation
metadata.pop_culture: The 'nothing' in The Neverending Story; the quantum vacuum in science documentaries

sections.Story:
To the south, a land of FLAME,
MUSPELHEIM was its burning name,
Where rivers ran with liquid fire
And sparks flew higher, ever higher.

To the north, a land of FROST,
NIFLHEIM, where all warmth was lost,
Where icy rivers slowly crept
And frozen mists forever slept.

Then fire and ice began to MEET
In the gap where cold kissed HEAT,
And from that place where opposites kissed,
Rose drops of life from melting MIST.
sections.Wonder: Fire and ice are opposites — but they made the world together! Can you think of other opposites that work better as a team?
sections.Whisper: The eleven rivers of Niflheim (the Élivágar) and the sparks of Muspelheim meeting in Ginnungagap is detailed in Gylfaginning 4-5. This cosmogony of creation through the meeting of opposites parallels Heraclitus's unity of opposites and Chinese yin-yang philosophy. The Norse cosmos begins with dialectic, not divine fiat.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

[Footnote 13: Elder Edda: The Vala’s Prophecy, 56.]

5. Said Ganglere: What took place before the races came into existence, and men increased and multiplied? Replied Har, explaining, that as soon as the streams, that are called the Elivogs, had come so far from their source that the venomous yeast which flowed with them hardened, as does dross that runs from the fire, then it turned into ice. And when this ice stopped and flowed no more, then gathered over it the drizzling rain that arose from the venom and froze into rime, and one layer of ice was laid upon the other clear into Ginungagap. Then said Jafnhar: All that part of Ginungagap that turns toward the north was filled with thick and heavy ice and rime, and everywhere within were drizzling rains and gusts. But the south part of Ginungagap was lighted up by the glowing sparks that flew out of Muspelheim. Added Thride: As cold and all things grim proceeded from Niflheim, so that which bordered on Muspelheim was hot and bright, and Ginungagap was as warm and mild as windless air.

metadata.archetype: Coincidentia Oppositorum — creation through the meeting of opposites
metadata.pop_culture: Frozen's ice magic vs. fire spirits in Frozen 2; Robert Frost's poem 'Fire and Ice'

sections.Story:
From melting ice a shape appeared,
A GIANT form — so huge and weird!
His name was YMIR, first to wake,
And when he moved, the whole void shook and quaked.

He slept and sweated in his dreams,
And from his sweat came living STREAMS —
More giants grew from arms and feet,
A family born from frost and HEAT.

So Ymir was the FIRST of all,
Before the mountains, great and tall,
Before the oceans, before the shore —
The very first who ever WORE a form of flesh and bone and breath,
The giant born from ice and death.
sections.Wonder: Ymir was all alone at first — the only being in the whole universe! Have you ever felt like you were the only one awake? Was it scary or exciting?
sections.Whisper: Ymir (also called Aurgelmir) is the primordial jötunn whose body becomes the raw material of creation. In Vafþrúðnismál 21, we learn beings grew from his sweat. The motif of a cosmic giant whose body becomes the world appears in Vedic tradition (Purusha in Rig Veda 10.90) and Chinese myth (Pangu), suggesting a deep Indo-European inheritance.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

OF THE HIGHEST GOD.

3. Ganglere then made the following question: Who is the highest and oldest of all the gods? Made answer Har: Alfather he is called in our tongue, but in Asgard of old he had twelve names. The first is Alfather, the second is Herran or Herjan, the third Nikar or Hnikar, the fourth Nikuz or Hnikud, the fifth Fjolner, the sixth Oske, the seventh Ome, the eighth Biflide or Biflinde, the ninth Svidar, the tenth Svidrer, the eleventh Vidrer, the twelfth Jalg or Jalk. Ganglere asks again: Where is this god? What can he do? What mighty works has he accomplished? Answered Har: He lives from everlasting to everlasting, rules over all his realm, and governs all things, great and small. Then remarked Jafnhar: He made heaven and earth, the air and all things in them. Thride added: What is most important, he made man and gave him a spirit, which shall live, and never perish, though the body may turn to dust or burn to ashes. All who live a life of virtue shall dwell with him in Gimle or Vingolf. The wicked, on the other hand, go to Hel, and from her to Niflhel, that is, down into the ninth world.

metadata.archetype: The Cosmic Giant — primordial being whose body becomes the world
metadata.pop_culture: Giants in Marvel's Thor; the Ice Giants in How to Train Your Dragon

sections.Story:
Beside the giant, licking ICE,
A great cow stood — so warm and NICE!
Her name was AUDHUMLA, salt-lick queen,
The gentlest beast you've ever seen.

She licked the ice blocks, day by day,
And slowly melted frost AWAY,
Until a FACE appeared inside —
A handsome god the ice would hide!

His name was BÚRI, strong and bright,
Licked free from ice into the LIGHT,
So from a cow and frozen streams
The GODS were born from salty dreams.
sections.Wonder: A cow licked the first god out of ice! What's the strangest way you can imagine something being born or discovered?
sections.Whisper: Auðumbla appears only in Gylfaginning 6. The sacred cow motif connects to Indo-European cattle worship — compare Vedic Kamadhenu and Egyptian Hathor. Búri, grandfather of Odin, being revealed through patient licking suggests that divine potential must be uncovered gradually, not created instantly. Creation as revelation rather than fabrication.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

[Footnote 46: Both these words mean sloth.]

35. The wolf was fostered by the asas at home, and Tyr was the only one who had the courage to go to him and give him food. When the gods saw how much he grew every day, and all prophecies declared that he was predestined to become fatal to them, they resolved to make a very strong fetter, which they called Lading. They brought it to the wolf, and bade him try his strength on the fetter. The wolf, who did not think it would be too strong for him, let them do therewith as they pleased. But as soon as he spurned against it the fetter burst asunder, and he was free from Lading. Then the asas made another fetter, by one-half stronger, and this they called Drome. They wanted the wolf to try this also, saying to him that he would become very famous for his strength, if so strong a chain was not able to hold him. The wolf thought that this fetter was indeed very strong, but also that his strength had increased since he broke Lading. He also took into consideration that it was necessary to expose one’s self to some danger if he desired to become famous; so he let them put the fetter on him.

metadata.archetype: The Nourishing Mother — life sustained by primal feminine force
metadata.pop_culture: Mother Earth figures in Moana; the nurturing creatures in Studio Ghibli films

sections.Story:
Now Búri had a son named BOR,
Who loved a giantess — and what's more,
She gave him THREE sons, brave and bold:
ODIN and VILI and VÉ, we're told.

Three brothers standing side by side,
They looked at Ymir's world and CRIED:
"This giant's chaos — frost and gloom —
We'll make it better! We'll make ROOM!"

So gods and giants shared the DARK,
But in the brothers burned a SPARK
Of something new — a plan, a DREAM
To shape the void into a gleam.
sections.Wonder: The three brothers wanted to make the world better. If you and your friends could remake the world, what's the first thing you'd change?
sections.Whisper: The three sons of Borr — Óðinn, Vili, and Vé — are named in Gylfaginning 6. Their names may encode aspects of consciousness: Óðinn (ecstasy/inspiration), Vili (will/intention), Vé (sacred space). The triune god-form appears across Indo-European traditions: Brahma-Vishnu-Shiva, Jupiter-Mars-Quirinus. Three brothers is the minimum unit of creative disagreement.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

"How did the race of Ymir spread itself?" asked Gangler; "or dost thou believe that this giant was a god?"

"We are far from believing him to have been a god," replied Har, "for he was wicked as are all of his race, whom we call Frost-giants. And it is said that, when Ymir slept, he fell into a sweat, and from the pit of his left arm was born a man and a woman, and one of his feet engendered with the other a son, from whom descend the Frost-giants, and we therefore call Ymir the old Frost-giant."

OF THE COW AUDHUMLA, AND THE BIRTH OF ODIN.

6. "Where dwelt Ymir, and on what did he live?" asked Gangler.

"Immediately after the gelid vapours had been resolved into drops," replied Kar, "there was formed out of them the cow named Audhumla. Four streams of milk ran from her teats, and thus fed she Ymir."

"But on what did the cow feed?" questioned Gangler.

"The cow," answered Har, "supported herself by licking the stones that were covered with salt and hoar frost. The first day that she licked these stones there sprang from them, towards evening, the hairs of a man, the second day a head, and on the third an entire man, who was endowed with beauty, agility and power.

metadata.archetype: The Divine Triad — three aspects of creative consciousness
metadata.pop_culture: Three brothers in Brave; three good fairies in Sleeping Beauty

sections.Story:
The brothers slew old YMIR dead
And from his body the world was SPREAD:
His FLESH became the soil and ground,
His BLOOD the seas that wrap around.

His BONES became the mountains high,
His SKULL was lifted for the SKY,
His BRAINS became the drifting clouds —
What once was giant now wore world-SHROUDS.

They called it MIDGARD, middle-earth,
A world of wonder, death, and BIRTH,
And set a fence of Ymir's BROW
To keep the giants out — for NOW.
sections.Wonder: The world is made from a giant's body — mountains are bones, the sky is a skull! If YOUR body were a world, what would your hair be? Your teeth?
sections.Whisper: The dismemberment of Ymir in Gylfaginning 8 is a classic 'world-body' cosmogony. The same motif appears in Rig Veda 10.90 (Purusha Sukta) where Purusha's body becomes the cosmos. 'Midgard' (Miðgarðr) means 'middle enclosure' — humans live in the protected center, with giants (chaos) outside. This is the mythic origin of Tolkien's 'Middle-earth.'
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

56. "I find by your account," said Gangler, "that Utgard-Loki possesses great might in himself, though he has recourse to spells and illusions; but his power may be seen by his followers, being in every respect so skillful and dexterous. But tell me, did Thor ever avenge this affront?"

"It is not unknown," replied Har, "though nobody has talked of it, that Thor was determined to make amends for the journey just spoken of, and he had not been long at home ere he set out again so hastily that he had neither his car nor his goats, nor any followers with him. He went out of Midgard under the semblance of a young man, and came at dusk to the dwelling of a giant called Hymir. Here Thor passed the night, but at break of day, when he perceived that Hymir was making his boat ready for fishing, he arose and dressed himself, and begged the giant would let him row out to sea with him. Hymir answered, that a puny stripling like he was could be of no great use to him.

metadata.archetype: The World-Body — cosmos formed from sacrifice of the primordial being
metadata.pop_culture: Middle-earth in Lord of the Rings; the worldbuilding in Minecraft

sections.Story:
At the center stands a TREE,
So tall that none can fully SEE
Its top among the highest STARS,
Its roots below all gates and bars.

Its name is YGGDRASIL the great,
Three ROOTS hold up the worlds and fate:
One drinks from wisdom's icy WELL,
One reaches up, one reaches to HEL.

A SQUIRREL runs from top to base,
An EAGLE watches from its place,
A SERPENT gnaws the deepest root —
The tree of life bears every FRUIT.
sections.Wonder: Yggdrasil holds ALL the worlds together — like a giant apartment building for the universe! If you lived in the World Tree, which branch would you choose?
sections.Whisper: Yggdrasil ('Odin's horse' — named because Odin hung upon it) appears extensively in Grímnismál 29-35 and Völuspá 2, 19. The three roots reach to Hel, to the frost-giants, and to humankind. Ratatoskr the squirrel carries insults between the eagle Veðrfölnir above and the serpent Níðhöggr below — a vertical axis mundi connecting all levels of being. Compare the Kabbalistic Tree of Life and the Buddhist Bodhi Tree.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

ON THE WONDERFUL THINGS IN HEAVEN.

15. Then said Ganglere: Where is the chief or most holy place of the gods? Har answered: That is by the ash Ygdrasil. There the gods meet in council every day. Said Ganglere: What is said about this place? Answered Jafnhar: This ash is the best and greatest of all trees; its branches spread over all the world, and reach up above heaven. Three roots sustain the tree and stand wide apart; one root is with the asas and another with the frost-giants, where Ginungagap formerly was; the third reaches into Niflheim; under it is Hvergelmer, where Nidhug gnaws the root from below. But under the second root, which extends to the frost-giants, is the well of Mimer, wherein knowledge and wisdom are concealed. The owner of the well hight Mimer. He is full of wisdom, for he drinks from the well with the Gjallar-horn. Alfather once came there and asked for a drink from the well, but he did not get it before he left one of his eyes as a pledge. So it is said in the Vala’s Prophecy:

Well know I, Odin, Where you hid your eye: In the crystal-clear Well of Mimer. Mead drinks Mimer Every morning From Valfather’s pledge.

metadata.archetype: The Axis Mundi — the cosmic center connecting all worlds
metadata.pop_culture: The World Tree in God of War; Groot in Guardians of the Galaxy; the Tree of Souls in Avatar

sections.Story:
An old man walks the dusty ROAD,
A wide-brimmed hat, a heavy LOAD,
One eye that gleams like morning STAR —
He's ODIN, wandering near and far.

He knocks on doors at close of DAY,
A stranger asking if he might STAY,
He tells such tales beside the FIRE
That no one thinks to call him liar.

But underneath that cloak and HOOD
Beats the heart of FATHERHOOD —
The king of gods walks paths of DIRT
To learn what wisdom can't be taught from any SHIRT.
sections.Wonder: Odin disguises himself as a wanderer to learn things! Have you ever learned something important from someone you didn't expect?
sections.Whisper: Odin as the wandering old man (Grímnismál, Vafþrúðnismál) is the direct ancestor of Tolkien's Gandalf — wide hat, cloak, staff, one eye. His over 200 names (heiti) in the Eddas reflect his shapeshifting nature. Odin is not a sky-father like Zeus but a shaman-king: restless, curious, willing to sacrifice himself for knowledge.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

About the name Edda there has also been much learned discussion. Some have suggested that it may be a mutilated form of the word Odde, the home of Sæmund the Wise, who was long supposed to be the compiler of the Elder Edda. In this connection, it has been argued that possibly Sæmund had begun the writing of the Younger Edda, too. Others derive the word from _óðr_ (mind, soul), which in poetical usage also means song, poetry. Others, again, connect Edda with the Sanscrit word Veda, which is supposed to mean knowledge. Finally, others adopt the meaning which the word has where it is actually used in the Elder Edda, and where it means great-grandmother. Vigfusson adopts this definition, and it is certainly both scientific and poetical. What can be more beautiful than the idea that our great ancestress teaches her descendants the sacred traditions, the concentrated wisdom, of the race? To sum up, then, we say the Younger, or Prose, or Snorre’s Edda has been produced at different times by various hands, and the object of its authors has been to produce a manual for the skalds.

metadata.archetype: The Wise Wanderer — the seeker who gains knowledge through journey
metadata.pop_culture: Gandalf in Lord of the Rings; Odin in Marvel's Thor; Wednesday in American Gods

sections.Story:
To learn the deepest, oldest LORE,
Odin chose what none had done BEFORE:
He HUNG himself upon the tree,
Nine days and nights — to set knowledge FREE.

The wind blew cold, no food, no DRINK,
Swaying on the BRINK,
He gave himself unto HIMSELF —
No book of wisdom on a shelf.

And on the ninth night, with a CRY,
He saw the RUNES before his eye!
Sacred letters, carved in SPACE —
The alphabet of every PLACE.
sections.Wonder: Odin didn't just read about wisdom — he suffered for it. What's something you worked really, really hard to learn? Was it worth it?
sections.Whisper: Hávamál 138-141 contains Odin's own account of his self-sacrifice: 'I know that I hung on a windswept tree / nine long nights.' This is a shamanic initiatory death — Odin sacrifices himself TO himself. Compare Christ's crucifixion, Attis hanging on a pine, and Siberian shamanic pole-climbing. The runes are not 'invented' but 'taken up' — discovered through suffering, not intellect.

sections.Story:
Beneath a root of Yggdrasil,
A WELL of wisdom, dark and STILL,
Old MÍMIR guards it, day and night —
Its waters grant the gift of SIGHT.

Odin came and asked to DRINK:
"One sip to help me truly THINK."
Mímir said, "The price is STEEP —
An EYE is what this well will keep."

Without a pause, without a TEAR,
Odin plucked his eye — so DEAR —
And dropped it in. The water GLEAMED.
Now he sees MORE than he ever dreamed.
sections.Wonder: Odin gave up one eye to see better with the other! Sometimes we have to give something up to gain something greater. What would you trade for the wisdom to understand everything?
sections.Whisper: Völuspá 28 and Gylfaginning 15 describe Mímir's Well (Mímisbrunnr) beneath the root of Yggdrasil that extends to the frost-giants. Odin's eye remains in the well, gazing upward — so the well sees through him and he sees through the well. This is not 'losing' sight but gaining second sight: trading binocular worldly vision for monocular visionary depth. Compare the Third Eye in Hindu tradition.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

"The wolf Fenrir advancing, opens his enormous mouth; the lower jaw reaches to the earth, and the upper one to heaven, and would in fact reach still farther were there space to admit of it. Fire flashes from his eyes and nostrils. The Midgard serpent, placing himself by the side of the wolf, vomits forth floods of poison which overwhelm the air and the waters. Amidst this devastation heaven is cleft in twain, and the sons of Muspell ride through the breach. Surtur rides first, and both before and behind him flames burning fire. His sword outshines the sun itself. Bifrost, as they ride over it, breaks to pieces. Then they direct their course to the battlefield called Vigrid. Thither also repair the wolf Fenrir and the Midgard serpent, and also Loki, with all the followers of Hel, and Hrym with all the Hrimthursar. But the sons of Muspell keep their effulgent bands apart on the field of battle, which is one hundred miles long on every side.

65. "Meanwhile Heimdall stands up, and with all his force sounds the Gjallar-horn to arouse the gods, who assemble without delay. Odin then rides to Mimir's well and consults Mimir how he and his warriors ought to enter into action.

metadata.archetype: The Sacred Exchange — giving up the mundane for the visionary
metadata.pop_culture: Odin's eye in Marvel's Thor; the Eye of Agamotto in Doctor Strange

sections.Story:
The RUNES are more than lines and DOTS,
More than scratches, more than KNOTS —
Each one SINGS a different song,
A piece of truth that's old and STRONG.

FEHU for cattle, wealth, and GOLD,
URUZ for the wild ox, brave and BOLD,
THURISAZ for Thor's own MIGHT —
Twenty-four runes burning BRIGHT.

Odin taught them to the WORLD,
And where the runes were carved or HURLED,
Magic woke in wood and STONE —
No one speaks a rune ALONE.
sections.Wonder: Runes aren't just letters — they're like tiny spells! If you could invent a magic symbol that meant one thing, what would your rune mean?
sections.Whisper: The Elder Futhark's 24 runes are detailed in the Rune Poems (Anglo-Saxon, Norwegian, Icelandic). Hávamál 144-163 lists the 18 charms Odin learned. The word 'rune' (rún) means 'secret' or 'mystery' in Old Norse. Runes were never merely an alphabet — they were a consciousness technology, each stave encoding a cosmic principle. Compare Hebrew letter-mysticism in Kabbalah and the Egyptian hieroglyphs as 'words of the gods.'
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

HOW NJORD GOT SKADE TO WIFE.

Skade, the daughter of the giant Thjasse, donned her helmet, and byrnie, and all her war-gear, and betook herself to Asgard to avenge her father’s death. The asas offered her ransom and atonement; and it was agreed to, in the first place, that she should choose herself a husband among the asas, but she was to make her choice by the feet, which was all she was to see of their persons. She saw one man’s feet that were wonderfully beautiful, and exclaimed: This one I choose! On Balder there are few blemishes. But it was Njord, from Noatun. In the second place, it was stipulated that the asas were to do what she did not deem them capable of, and that was to make her laugh. Then Loke tied one end of a string fast to the beard of a goat and the other around his own body, and one pulled this way and the other that, and both of them shrieked out loud. Then Loke let himself fall on Skade’s knees, and this made her laugh. It is said that Odin did even more than was asked, in that he took Thjasse’s eyes and cast them up into heaven, and made two stars of them.

metadata.archetype: Sacred Script — letters as containers of cosmic power
metadata.pop_culture: Rune magic in Frozen 2; the dwarven runes in The Hobbit; enchanted alphabets in Harry Potter

sections.Story:
Two RAVENS sit on Odin's throne,
Black as night and sharp as BONE:
HUGINN is Thought, who flies so fast,
MUNINN is Memory, holding the past.

Each morning when the sun turns RED,
They fly from Odin's shoulder and HEAD,
Across the NINE worlds, high and low,
To see what every creature KNOWS.

At evening they return to SPEAK,
Whispering secrets, beak to CHEEK.
Odin listens, nods, and SIGHS:
"I fear for Thought — but Memory FLIES
further than any fear can reach."
Two birds, two gifts, two things they TEACH.
sections.Wonder: Odin worries more about losing his Thought than his Memory. Which would YOU rather keep — the ability to think new things, or the ability to remember old things?
sections.Whisper: Grímnismál 20: 'Huginn and Muninn fly every day / over the wide world. / I fear for Huginn that he may not return, / yet more am I worried for Muninn.' This verse reveals Odin's deepest anxiety: that consciousness (Huginn) might be lost, but memory (Muninn) is even more precious. Compare the Aboriginal Australian concept of the Dreaming — memory as the fundament of reality itself.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

39. "If it be as thou hast told me," said Gangler, "that all men who have fallen in fight since the beginning of the world are gone to Odin, in Valhalla, what has he to give them to eat, for methinks there must be a great crowd there?"

"What thou sayest is quite true," replied Har, "the crowd there is indeed great, but great though it be, it will still increase, and will be thought too little when the wolf cometh. But however great the band of men in Valhalla may be, the flesh of the boar Sæhrimnir will more than suffice for their sustenance. For although this boar is sodden every morning he becomes whole again every night. But there are few, methinks, who are wise enough to give thee, in this respect, a satisfactory answer to thy question. The cook is called Andhrimnir, and the kettle Eldhrimnir. As it is said,--'Andhrimnir cooks in Eldhrimnir, Sæhrimnir.' 'Tis the best of flesh, though few know how much is required for the Einherjar."

"But has Odin," said Gangler, "the same food as the heroes?"

"Odin,' replied Har, 'gives the meat that is set before him to two wolves, called Geri and Freki, for he himself stands in no need of food.

sections.Story:
High above the clouds there gleams
A golden hall of warrior DREAMS —
VALHALLA, where the bravest GO
When they fall in battle down BELOW.

The VALKYRIES ride through storm and rain,
Choosing heroes from the SLAIN,
On flying horses, bright as LIGHTNING,
Beautiful and truly FRIGHTENING.

In Valhalla's hall, the chosen FEAST,
They fight all day then rest at LEAST,
And every wound is HEALED by night —
They're training for the final FIGHT.
sections.Wonder: The warriors train every single day for a battle at the end of the world. What would YOU want to practice every day if you knew the biggest challenge of your life was coming?
sections.Whisper: Valhöll ('hall of the slain') is described in Grímnismál 8-10 — 540 doors, each wide enough for 800 warriors. But Valhalla isn't paradise: it's a training camp. The einherjar practice-fight each day for Ragnarök, knowing they will lose. This is Norse existentialism: meaning comes not from victory but from the willingness to show up for an unwinnable battle. Compare the Bhagavad Gita's Arjuna facing inevitable war.

"The first and eldest of the Æsir," continued Thridi, "is Odin. He governs all things, and, although the other deities are powerful, they all serve and obey him as children do their father. Frigga is his wife. She foresees the destinies of men, but never reveals what is to come. For thus it is said that Odin himself told Loki, 'Senseless Loki, why wilt thou pry into futurity, Frigga alone knoweth the destinies of all, though she telleth them never?'

"Odin is named Alfadir (All-father), because he is the father of all the gods, and also Valfadir (Choosing Father), because he chooses for his sons all of those who fall in combat. Odin is also called Hangagud, Haptagud, and Farmagud, and, besides these, was named in many ways when he went to King Geirraudr," forty-nine names in all.

"A great many names, indeed!" exclaimed Gangler; "surely that man must be very wise who knows them all distinctly, and can tell on what occasions they were given."

sections.Story:
There once was a MEAD so sweet and rare,
One sip and you'd write poems beyond COMPARE,
The dwarves had brewed it — dark and DEEP —
From magic blood, a secret to KEEP.

A giant stole it, locked it TIGHT
Inside a mountain, out of SIGHT.
But Odin drilled into the STONE,
Turned into a SERPENT, slithered alone.

He drank the mead in three great SIPS,
Became an EAGLE, let it drip
From beak to Asgard's waiting BOWL —
Now every poet shares that SOUL.
sections.Wonder: The mead of poetry means that every time someone writes or tells a beautiful story, they're sharing something magical. When have YOU felt magic while listening to a story?
sections.Whisper: The myth of Kvasir's blood and the mead of poetry (Skáldskaparmál 1-2) is a foundational myth of artistic inspiration. Kvasir was born from the mixed saliva of the Æsir and Vanir — wisdom born from reconciliation. The mead's theft by Odin in eagle-form, spilling drops for mortal poets, explains why inspiration feels like something received, not manufactured. Compare the Greek Muses and the Hindu concept of Saraswati.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

70. "On his return to Asgard, Loki told Iduna that, in a forest at a short distance from the celestial residence, he had found apples growing which he thought were of a much better quality than her own, and that at all events it was worth while making a comparison between them. Iduna, deceived by his words, took her apples, and went with him into the forest, but they had no sooner entered it than Thjassi, clad in his eagle-plumage, flew rapidly towards them, and catching up Iduna, carried her treasure off with him to Jotunheim. The gods being thus deprived of their renovating apples, soon became wrinkled and grey; old age was creeping fast upon them, when they discovered that Loki had been, as usual, the contriver of all the mischief that had befallen them. They therefore threatened him with condign punishment if he did not instantly hit upon some expedient for bringing back Iduna and her apples to Asgard.

sections.Story:
In the forge beneath the GROUND,
Dwarves were hammering, pound by POUND,
Sparks flew up like orange RAIN
As they shaped the gods' most famous BANE.

MJÖLNIR — the hammer, short of HAFT,
(Loki's fault — that trickster LAUGHED
When a fly bit the dwarf mid-SWING
And the handle came out a tiny THING.)

But oh, that hammer hits so TRUE,
It flies out and comes back TO YOU,
It CRACKS the sky with thunder's sound —
The mightiest weapon ever FOUND.
sections.Wonder: Mjölnir isn't perfect — its handle is too short because of a mistake! Have you ever made something that turned out a little wrong but still worked great?
sections.Whisper: Skáldskaparmál 35 describes the forging of Mjölnir by the dwarves Sindri and Brokkr, with Loki (as a fly) sabotaging the bellows. The short handle is a 'flaw' that makes it MORE powerful — it must be wielded close, requiring courage. Mjölnir means 'crusher' or 'grinder' (related to Russian molniya, 'lightning'). Thor's hammer consecrated marriages, births, and funerals — it was a tool of blessing, not just destruction.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

THOR AND HRUNGNER.

Brage told Æger that Thor had gone eastward to crush trolls. Odin rode on his horse Sleipner to Jotunheim, and came to the giant whose name is Hrungner. Then asked Hrungner what man that was who with a golden helmet rode both through the air and over the sea, and added that he had a remarkably good horse. Odin said that he would wager his head that so good a horse could not be found in Jotunheim. Hrungner admitted that it was indeed an excellent horse, but he had one, called Goldfax, that could take much longer paces; and in his wrath he immediately sprang upon his horse and galloped after Odin, intending to pay him for his insolence. Odin rode so fast that he was a good distance ahead, but Hrungner had worked himself into such a giant rage that, before he was aware of it, he had come within the gates of Asgard. When he came to the hall door, the asas invited him to drink with them. He entered the hall and requested a drink. They then took the bowls that Thor was accustomed to drink from, and Hrungner emptied them all. When he became drunk, he gave the freest vent to his loud boastings.

sections.Story:
Thor packed his goats and chariot BRIGHT,
And rode to JÖTUNHEIM to fight!
The land of GIANTS, cold and vast,
Where every mountain watches as you pass.

He stopped at a farmer's humble HOME,
And shared his goats — down to the BONE!
"Just save the skins," Thor said with CARE,
"And I'll make them whole — beyond REPAIR."

Come morning, Thor blessed each goat HIDE,
They leapt up, living, at his SIDE!
(Though one limped a bit — Þjálfi's CRIME:
He'd cracked a bone at supper TIME.)
sections.Wonder: Thor shared everything he had but asked for one simple rule to be followed. Has anyone ever trusted you with a rule? Did you keep it?
sections.Whisper: Gylfaginning 44 tells of Thor's journey east with Loki, and the farmer's son Þjálfi cracking a goat-bone for marrow. Thor's goats Tanngrisnir and Tanngnjóstr ('teeth-barer' and 'teeth-grinder') regenerate from their hides — an agricultural resurrection myth. Þjálfi's marrow-cracking is a taboo violation (compare Prometheus stealing fire): divine gifts come with rules, and breaking them has consequences.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

[Footnote 60: Bonde = peasant.]

46. He left his goats there and went on his way east into Jotunheim, clear to the sea, and then he went on across the deep ocean, and went ashore on the other side, together with Loke and Thjalfe and Roskva. When they had proceeded a short distance, there stood before them a great wood, through which they kept going the whole day until dark. Thjalfe, who was of all men the fleetest of foot, bore Thor’s bag, but the wood was no good place for provisions. When it had become dark, they sought a place for their night lodging, and found a very large hall. At the end of it was a door as wide as the hall. Here they remained through the night. About midnight there was a great earthquake; the ground trembled beneath them, and the house shook. Then Thor stood up and called his companions. They looked about them and found an adjoining room to the right, in the midst of the hall, and there they went in. Thor seated himself in the door; the others went farther in and were very much frightened. Thor held his hammer by the handle, ready to defend himself. Then they heard a great groaning and roaring.

sections.Story:
Thor went fishing with a GIANT,
Hymir, grumpy and defiant.
"You're too SMALL to fish," he sneered.
Thor just smiled and pulled his BEARD.

He baited his hook with an ox's HEAD,
Cast it deep in the ocean's BED,
And something BIT — so huge, so strong,
The boat was dragged and pulled ALONG!

Up rose JÖRMUNGANDR, the world-wrapping SNAKE,
The sea did BOIL, the sky did SHAKE!
Thor raised his hammer — but Hymir CUT
The line! The serpent sank. Thor's eyes stayed SHUT
with rage — they'd meet again one DAY.
The final time, there'd be no getting AWAY.
sections.Wonder: Thor almost caught the biggest creature in the whole world! Have you ever almost done something amazing but were stopped at the last second? How did that feel?
sections.Whisper: Hymiskviða and Gylfaginning 48 give the fishing episode — one of the most depicted scenes in Norse art (Altuna runestone, c. 1050). Thor and Jörmungandr are fated enemies who will destroy each other at Ragnarök. This fishing trip is a premature confrontation: destiny delayed but not avoided. Hymir cutting the line represents the universe maintaining its timeline. Compare Ahab and the white whale.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

56. "I find by your account," said Gangler, "that Utgard-Loki possesses great might in himself, though he has recourse to spells and illusions; but his power may be seen by his followers, being in every respect so skillful and dexterous. But tell me, did Thor ever avenge this affront?"

"It is not unknown," replied Har, "though nobody has talked of it, that Thor was determined to make amends for the journey just spoken of, and he had not been long at home ere he set out again so hastily that he had neither his car nor his goats, nor any followers with him. He went out of Midgard under the semblance of a young man, and came at dusk to the dwelling of a giant called Hymir. Here Thor passed the night, but at break of day, when he perceived that Hymir was making his boat ready for fishing, he arose and dressed himself, and begged the giant would let him row out to sea with him. Hymir answered, that a puny stripling like he was could be of no great use to him.

sections.Story:
Wrapped around the world like a living BELT,
JÖRMUNGANDR sleeps where oceans MELT
Into the edge of everything —
A SERPENT biting its own tail-RING.

Loki's child, thrown in the SEA
By Odin ("Too dangerous to be FREE!"),
It GREW and grew till tail met HEAD —
Now if it lets go, the world is DEAD.

So there it lies, a CIRCLE round,
Holding everything safe and SOUND,
The monster that we need to STAY —
The snake that keeps the sea at BAY.
sections.Wonder: The thing that seems scariest — a giant snake! — is actually holding the world together. Can you think of something that seems scary but is actually keeping things safe?
sections.Whisper: Jörmungandr (Miðgarðsormr, 'Midgard Serpent') is the ouroboros of Norse myth — the self-consuming serpent encircling the world. When it releases its tail, Ragnarök begins. This is the paradox of containment: the 'monster' IS the world-boundary. Compare the Egyptian serpent Apophis circling the underworld, and the Aztec Quetzalcoatl as world-serpent. The ouroboros symbolizes cyclical time — endings that are beginnings.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

34. "There is another deity," continued Har, "reckoned in the number of the Æsir, whom some call the calumniator of the gods, the contriver of all fraud and mischief, and the disgrace of gods and men. His name is Loki or Loptur. He is the son of the giant Farbauti. His mother is Laufey or Nal; his brothers are Byleist and Helblindi. Loki is handsome and well made, but of a very fickle mood, and most evil disposition. He surpasses all beings in those arts called Cunning and Perfidy. Many a time has he exposed the gods to very great perils, and often extricated them again by his artifices. His wife is called Siguna, and their son Nari.

35. "Loki," continued Har, "has likewise had three children by Angurbodi, a giantess of Jotunheim. The first is the wolf Fenrir; the second Jormungand, the Midgard serpent; the third Hela (Death). The gods were not long ignorant that these monsters continued to be bred up in Jotunheim, and, having had recourse to divination, became aware of all the evils they would have to suffer from them; their being sprung from such a mother was a bad presage, and from such a sire was still worse.

sections.Story:
A giant stole Thor's hammer one DAY
And wouldn't give it back, no WAY!
"I'll return it," Thrym said with a GRIN,
"When Freya comes to MARRY me — let the wedding BEGIN!"

Freya said NO with a volcanic ROAR,
So Loki had a plan in STORE:
"Thor, put on a DRESS and veil —
YOU'LL be the bride! It cannot FAIL!"

So Thor in a wedding gown showed UP,
Ate eight salmon and drank each CUP,
And when they brought the hammer to BLESS
The bride — Thor ripped away the DRESS
And SMASHED every giant in the hall!
The funniest rescue of them ALL.
sections.Wonder: Thor dressed up as a bride to get his hammer back! Sometimes the silliest plan is the best plan. What's the goofiest idea you ever had that actually worked?
sections.Whisper: Þrymskviða is one of the most beloved Eddic poems — a comedy in a mythological tradition that tends toward tragedy. Thor's cross-dressing challenges rigid gender performance: the mightiest warrior achieves victory through disguise and deception, traditionally 'feminine' tactics. Loki as wedding planner is in his element. The poem may preserve pre-Christian ritual elements of sacred gender-crossing in Germanic practice.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

35. They had far journeyed before Odin's son cast one look backward: he from the caverns saw, with Hymir from the east, a troop of many-headed monsters coming.

36. From his shoulders he lifted the kettle down; Miollnir hurled forth towards the savage crew, and slew all the mountain-giants, who with Hymir had him pursued.

37. Long they had not journeyed when of Hlorridi's goats one lay down half-dead before the car. It from the pole had sprung across the trace; but the false Loki was of this the cause.

38. Now ye have heard,--for what fabulist can more fully tell--what indemnity he from the giant got: he paid for it with his children both.[32]

sections.Story:
Thor came to UTGARD, giants' keep,
Where the king said, "Show your strength — or SLEEP!"
Thor tried to DRINK a horn dry —
But the ocean dropped! (The horn was the SEA in disguise!)

He tried to LIFT a giant's cat —
But it was the World Serpent, COILED flat!
He wrestled an OLD woman frail —
But she was OLD AGE, beyond the pale!

The giant king said, "Now you SEE:
You drank the ocean, just not ALL of the sea,
You lifted the SERPENT almost free,
You nearly beat DEATH — that's strong to ME!"
Thor's failures were the greatest deeds of ALL —
Even giants trembled at his NEAR-fall.
sections.Wonder: Thor 'lost' every challenge — but the giants were terrified of how close he came! Have you ever 'failed' at something but realized you actually did something amazing?
sections.Whisper: Gylfaginning 45-47 presents the Útgarða-Loki episode — the most philosophically sophisticated Thor myth. Every 'contest' is an illusion: Thor drinks the ocean, lifts the World Serpent, wrestles Old Age (Elli). His 'failures' terrify the giants because they reveal how close a god can come to overcoming cosmic forces. This is Norse existentialism: heroism is measured not by winning but by the scale of what you attempt.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

48. At daybreak the next day Thor and his companions arose, dressed themselves and were ready to depart. Then came Utgard-Loke and had the table spread for them, and there was no lack of feasting both in food and in drink. When they had breakfasted, they immediately departed from the burg. Utgard-Loke went with them out of the burg, but at parting he spoke to Thor and asked him how he thought his journey had turned out, or whether he had ever met a mightier man than himself. Thor answered that he could not deny that he had been greatly disgraced in this meeting; and this I know, he added, that you will call me a man of little account, whereat I am much mortified. Then said Utgard-Loke: Now I will tell you the truth, since you have come out of the burg, that if I live, and may have my way, you shall never enter it again; and this I know, forsooth, that you should never have come into it had I before known that you were so strong, and that you had come so near bringing us into great misfortune. Know, then, that I have deceived you with illusions.

metadata.archetype: The Noble Failure — heroism measured by the scale of what is attempted
metadata.pop_culture: Impossible challenges in The Labyrinth; 'it's not about winning' themes in Kung Fu Panda

sections.Story:
While Odin sits on his golden THRONE,
Thor walks the earth, never ALONE,
He guards the farmers, guards the POOR,
The common folk — that's what he's FOR.

When thunder ROLLS across the sky,
The Vikings knew that Thor was NIGH,
His chariot pulled by goats of MIGHT,
Crashing through the stormy NIGHT.

He doesn't seek the sage's LORE
Or wisdom's well — but something MORE:
The strength to STAND for those in need —
The simplest and the greatest DEED.
sections.Wonder: Odin is the god of kings and wise people, but Thor is the god of regular farmers and families. Who do you think the world needs more — someone super wise or someone super strong and kind?
sections.Whisper: Thor was the most popular Norse deity — more place-names, more amulets, more personal names (Þorsteinn, Þorbjörn) than any other god. Adam of Bremen (c. 1070) describes Thor's prominence at the Temple of Uppsala. While Odin belongs to the aristocratic warrior class, Thor is the people's god — protector of Midgard, defender against chaos. His worship persisted longest after Christianization precisely because common people loved him most.
sections.Original:
— Snorri Sturluson, The Prose Edda (Rasmus Anderson, trans.) —

It is a deplorable fact that the religion of our forefathers seems to be but little cared for in this country. The mythologies of other nations every student manifests an interest for. He reads with the greatest zeal all the legends of Rome and Greece, of India and China. He is familiar with every room in the labyrinth of Crete, while when he is introduced to the shining halls of Valhal and Gladsheim he gropes his way like a blind man. He does not know that Idun, with her beautiful apples, might, if applied to, render even greater services than Ariadne with her wonderful thread. When we inquire whom Tuesday and Wednesday and Thursday and Friday are named after, and press questions in reference to Tyr, Odin, Thor and Freyja, we get at best but a wise and knowing look. Are we, then, as a nation, like the ancient Jews, and do we bend the knee before the gods of foreign nations and forsake the altars of our own gods? What if we then should suffer the fate of that unhappy people--be scattered over all the world and lose our fatherland? In these Eddas our fathers have bequeathed unto us all their profoundest, all their sublimest, all their best thought.

metadata.source: Gylfaginning 21; Adam of Bremen, Gesta Hammaburgensis IV.26
metadata.archetype: The People's Champion — divine power in service of the ordinary

sections.Story:
Nobody knows where LOKI came from —
Half god, half giant, belonging to NONE,
A face that SHIFTS like fire in wind,
A smile that could charm you — or make you SKINNED.

He's been a SALMON, swift and bright,
A FLY that buzzes, a MARE at night,
An old WOMAN, a flickering FLAME —
No shape too strange, no form too TAME.

The gods all need him, though they FROWN,
He builds them up then tears them DOWN,
The TRICKSTER dancing on the edge —
A knife that cuts both sides of the LEDGE.
sections.Wonder: Loki can become anything — a fish, a fly, a horse! If you could shapeshift into any animal for one day, which would you pick and why?
sections.Whisper: Loki (Loptr, Hveðrungr) defies categories: he is a jötunn's son sworn as Odin's blood-brother (Lokasenna 9). His shapeshifting includes female forms — as a mare he births Sleipnir (Gylfaginning 42). Loki is not 'evil' in the Christian sense but amoral — a boundary-crosser. Compare Coyote in Navajo tradition, Anansi in West African tradition, and Hermes in Greek myth. The trickster exists wherever rigid categories need disrupting.

38. Gymer hight a man whose wife was Orboda, of the race of the mountain giants. Their daughter was Gerd, the fairest of all women. One day when Frey had gone into Hlidskjalf, and was looking out upon all the worlds, he saw toward the north a hamlet wherein was a large and beautiful house. To this house went a woman, and when she raised her hands to open the door, both the sky and the sea glistened therefrom, and she made all the world bright. As a punishment for his audacity in seating himself in that holy seat, Frey went away full of grief. When he came home, he neither spake, slept, nor drank, and no one dared speak to him. Then Njord sent for Skirner, Frey’s servant, bade him go to Frey and ask him with whom he was so angry, since he would speak to nobody. Skirner said that he would go, though he was loth to do so, as it was probable that he would get evil words in reply. When he came to Frey and asked him why he was so sad that he would not talk, Frey answered that he had seen a beautiful woman, and for her sake he had become so filled with grief, that he could not live any longer if he could not get her.

sections.Story:
Loki loved a giantess of the EAST,
And fathered children — each a BEAST:
FENRIR the wolf, with jaws so WIDE,
JÖRMUNGANDR the snake, and pale HEL beside.

The gods grew frightened of this BROOD:
The wolf was chained (though that seemed RUDE),
The snake was thrown into the SEA,
And Hel was sent where dead souls PLEA.

But here's the twist the Norns had SPUN:
These 'monsters' each will have their FUN —
The wolf eats Odin, the snake kills THOR,
And Hel keeps ALL who die in war.
The children no one WANTED near
Bring the end that all gods FEAR.
sections.Wonder: The gods were scared of Loki's children and sent them away. Do you think things would have been different if they'd been kinder? Does being mean to someone who's different ever make things better?
sections.Whisper: Gylfaginning 34 describes the binding of Fenrir, casting out of Jörmungandr, and banishment of Hel — all children of Loki and the giantess Angrboða ('grief-bringer'). The gods' fear creates the very enemies who destroy them — a self-fulfilling prophecy. This is the Norse understanding of fate (örlög): not external decree but the consequences of one's own actions rippling forward. Oppression breeds the revolution that overthrows the oppressor.
